title: Unfolding
tagline: VR cinematic experiment to develop narrative conventions
description: A 180° 3D stereoscopic VR short film exploring what narrative conventions emerge when the viewer is placed inside the story space, shot and edited in Unity.
what: A 180° 3D stereoscopic VR short film shot and edited in Unity, exploring what narrative conventions emerge when the viewer is placed inside the story space. The piece unfolds in a single domestic interior, using spatial audio and eye-level framing to create presence without interactivity.
why: Most VR narrative work either borrows from film or from games. This project asked what a third thing might look like — conventions native to the medium, emerging from constraints rather than borrowed from adjacent forms.
awards: Barcelona Planet Film Festival — Best VR 2025
Miami Art Tech Summit Official Selection 2025

title: unstudio
tagline: Spatial intervention at Harvard GSD — a collision space for 350+ students without studio
description: A semester-long course platform at Harvard GSD, designed as a collision space for students across programs who lack dedicated studio space.
what: A pilot collaborative space for the third of GSD students who have no home base in Gund Hall. Running April 11–20, 2026, the unstudio transformed an underutilized room into a drop-in collision space using furniture that already existed. 15 people at once at peak occupancy. 84 community members added their voices. 7 events hosted in 8 days.
why: Gund Hall was designed for 400 students and currently holds 994. Over 370 students have no desk in the school. 89% of existing desks sit empty at any given time. The cultural belief — that desks are only for studio — was excluding a third of students from their creative home base.

title: GRE Admissions Study
tagline: Survey showing 56% of MDes students wouldn't have applied with GRE — reversed the decision
description: Survey, interview, and admissions data analysis across 12 graduate design programs examining how standardized testing requirements correlate with applicant diversity.
what: Survey, interview, and admissions data analysis across 12 graduate design programs, examining how standardized testing requirements correlate with applicant diversity along axes of race, income, and first-generation status.
why: Admissions criteria are often treated as neutral but shape who enters a field. This study aimed to give programs evidence to inform policy changes.

title: Mediums Proseminar
tagline: Media theory seminar for MDes students, co-led with Allen Sayegh at Harvard GSD
description: A media theory seminar for incoming MDes students, co-led with domain head Allen Sayegh, pairing critical theory with hands-on experiential learning in different media.
what: Co-led the Mediums domain seminar with Allen Sayegh for incoming MDes students. Paired critical design readings with experiential learning: students visited the Harvard glass flower collection comparing simulacra with originals; responded to Berger's Ways of Seeing by making short films in the Fogg Art Museum for an in-class festival; shared personal design practices for critique, leading to unexpected collaborations and the first sale of one student's work to another.
why: If the medium is the message (McLuhan), the pedagogical challenge is how to render the medium visible and pliable. By teaching about each medium through a combination of mediums, students gained the agency to extend their practices by understanding each one's unique affordances.

title: MDes NPS Study
tagline: NPS survey of Harvard MDes alumni — score of -16, driving program redesign
description: A longitudinal study of alumni satisfaction across five cohorts of the Harvard MDes program, using NPS and qualitative interviews.
what: NPS survey of 129 MDes students returned a score of -16 (22% Promoters, 38% Detractors), compared to Harvard Business School's +37. Of 108 students who left comments, the program's core strength — radical curricular flexibility and access to the Harvard/MIT ecosystem — is widely praised even by Detractors. But three structural complaints drag the score down: under-resourced relative to cost, no dedicated physical space and community in GSD, and lack of mentorship for students who need scaffolding.
why: MDes is counter-cultural within Harvard, and improbable within the GSD. That's why I'm driven to help it thrive — I can imagine MDes being truly great, a profoundly effective teaching moment for generations of designers. And I want to live in the world that its students eventually design.

title: How to Change Institutions
tagline: A practical field guide for designers working inside large organizations
description: A short illustrated guide to changing institutions through care, collaboration, and mindfulness — an alternative to adversarial activism.
what: A 45-page illustrated guide for people who work inside institutions — schools, governments, hospitals — and want to create lasting change through collaboration rather than conflict. Covers how to notice what you want to change, how to work with institutional complexity, how to build coalitions through individual relationships, and how to sustain momentum. Illustrated by Julia Mattis. In homage to Thich Nhat Hanh.
why: Most change-making assumes adversarial tactics. This book explores what becomes possible when you choose collaboration over antagonism — assuming people within institutions mutually care for each other, and inviting them to be their best selves together.

title: SplatCubes
tagline: Gaussian splat → 3D printed physical sculpture via Grasshopper and C#
description: A workflow for converting 3D Gaussian Splat captures into physical 3D-printed sculptures.
what: A novel computational design pipeline for converting 3D Gaussian Splat captures of reality into printable forms, fabricated on multi-material resin printers. Uses custom Grasshopper scripts in C# to translate the digital representation of a Gaussian Splat into a physical artifact that can be held, viewed from any angle, and appreciated as a form of tangible volumetric memory.
why: Gaussian Splatting captures scenes as probabilistic, spatially distributed semi-opaque volumes — more accurate approximations of physical reality than mesh topology. But splats remain inherently digital. This pipeline physically manifests splats via 3D printing, emphasizing the blurriness and continuity of real-world boundaries that hard mesh edges erase.

title: HandMove
tagline: Unity package for Meta Quest: finger-pinch gesture teleportation without controllers
description: A Unity-based hand-tracking interaction prototype for Meta Quest with 12 custom gesture primitives, user-tested with 20 participants.
what: A Unity package for the Meta Quest enabling controller-free VR locomotion through a finger-pinch gesture mechanic. Extends Meta's All-In-One SDK with a custom C# script, prefabs, and a sample scene. Uses hand-tracking data to detect finger proximity, computes a parabolic arc toward the target surface, and smoothly transitions the camera rig. Special attention paid to reducing motion sickness: the arc preview provides spatial context before teleporting, and the transition is deliberately instantaneous rather than animated.
why: Most VR teleportation schemes are clunky, controller-dependent, and cognitively heavy — they force the user to think about navigation rather than inhabiting the virtual space. Handmove reduces this to a minimum: pinch your fingers together and a teleport arc appears; release to move. Publicly available as a Unity package designed for minimal integration.

title: Sorry
tagline: Interactive web app that teaches you how to give a healing apology
description: An interactive, completely private, locally-cached web app that teaches you the rules for writing a healing apology.
what: Built in ReactJS. Step-by-step process distilled from Brené Brown and Harriet Lerner's research on what makes an apology effective. Private, locally-cached, free to use.
why: Skillful apologies can build and repair relationships, but the research is hard to remember in the moment. An interactive digital tool is a better platform than a podcast for delivering the training.

title: Tonos
tagline: Music-based meditation app — find the sound of your feelings
description: A music-based meditation app that helps you find the sound of your feelings and process them more effectively.
what: Voice-guided app that walks you through identifying your emotional states and translates them into music — over a thousand possible combinations. Built with Luis Zanforlin.
why: Music is a powerful tool for emotion regulation. This app lets you compose music that matches your emotional state, making the science of mood repair interactive.

title: Arena of Wants
tagline: Locally-cached web app where you choose what you want most
description: A locally-cached web app for surfacing and ranking your wants.
what: A locally-cached web app at arenaofwants.com. Users enter their wants and the app surfaces them in head-to-head comparisons, building a ranked list of what you want most.
why: A core tenet in constructionism is learner agency. But people often feel constrained by others' expectations, unable to act in ways aligned with their core desires. Constructive-developmental theory (Kegan, 1980) suggests most adults operate at a "socialized" stage, subject to others' norms. Arena is a self-authoring intervention that reframes constraints as competing wants and helps users reprioritize to act most in line with their own goals.

title: Athletics Canada
tagline: Growth mindset film for para-athletes, televised to 7.2 million people
description: Psychology-informed short film empowering paralympic youth, commissioned by Athletics Canada and televised to 7.2 million people during the 2016 Paralympics.
what: Athletics Canada commissioned this short film, televised to 7.2 million people during the 2016 Paralympics, to inspire and recruit the next generation of Canadian para-athletes. Shot on RED cinema cameras with documentary-style direction and intercut with archival footage. Produced at Maku Creative.
why: People with growth mindsets interpret failure as proof they need to work harder. Research shows mindsets can shift through targeted messaging. This was an experiment in applied social psychology — designing a storyboard around scientific literature and using television to deliver a growth mindset intervention to millions of children.

title: Crawl
tagline: 20-foot handwritten shirt crawled through Harvard Yard — a free speech performance
description: Performance art in response to Harvard ranking last out of 258 universities in the 2024 free speech rankings.
what: Harvard ranked last out of 258 universities in the 2024 free speech rankings. Roy handwrote the entire 2,121-word Harvard Campus Use Rules on a 20-foot shirt and crawled through Harvard Yard and up the Widener Library steps. Inspired by the work of Pope.L.
why: Disseminating the university's own words to its own community — a mirror held up to the Rules and the fear they encode, on both sides.

title: Taxonomy of Narrative Design
tagline: Classification system for narrative perspective: 0th, 1st, 2nd, and 3rd person
description: A research framework classifying narrative mediums by the perspective the story gives the viewer, as a practical design tool for selecting the most effective form.
what: A two-axis classification system for narrative mediums based on the perspective the story gives the viewer. The key test: would the viewer say 'they went home' (3rd person), 'you fight the zombies' (2nd), 'I built a city' (1st), or 'Go here' (0th person)?
why: Different levels of visual fidelity and viewer agency correspond to different narrative forms. This system is a design tool for selecting the qualities that will make a story most effective.

title: Mu Mirror
tagline: p5.js webcam dot-grid — a Zen koan about the boundary of self
description: A p5.js webcam installation that dissolves your reflection into a field of moving dots.
what: Built in p5.js. Reads webcam data, compares pixels between frames by color similarity, and moves each pixel-dot toward its closest match — lerping both location and color over time. The result: your reflection dissolves and re-forms as a living dot-field.
why: Zen koans use riddles to trigger insight. This piece tries to make non-duality felt in the body rather than understood intellectually — if your reflection is made of the same dots as everything around you, where do you end?

title: Digital Typewriter
tagline: No-backspace writing tool — commit to every word
description: Write for keeps. No backspace. No undo.
what: Browser app in vanilla JS. No delete, no backspace, no undo. Characters are permanent. A tool for writing with commitment rather than endless revision.
why: Digital Typewriter is one of several projects exploring constraints as creative catalysts — the proposal that limitation is not always the enemy of expression, and that sometimes the best way to unlock creativity is to remove the ability to second-guess it. An attempt to bring David Pye's 'workmanship of risk' back to digital media, and a solution to Walter Benjamin's struggles with art in the age of mechanical reproduction.

title: Matchstick
tagline: SDF 3D-printed flame sculpture
description: A 3D-printed flame modeled as a signed distance function in Rhino, Grasshopper, and Blender.
what: A 3D-printed flame built using a signed distance field (SDF) in Rhino/Grasshopper. A voxel grid is populated with two volumes (a sphere for the match head, a conical swept curve for the flame tip) and the SDF measures each voxel's distance to the nearest surface. Cubes instantiated at voxels inside the flame are scaled proportionally to their SDF value — creating a soft, continuous boundary, the mathematical equivalent of how real flames blur into air. Exported as separate STLs per material layer for multi-material resin printing. One surface left unsanded after noticing the print striations create a moiré effect that makes the flame appear to flicker.
why: The project is a meditation on translation: what gets preserved and what gets lost when an idea moves from mathematics to matter.

title: Rehoboth
tagline: LiDAR hologram VR experience — a walkable memory of a place and its people
description: A LiDAR hologram-based virtual memory of the elderly that family members can step back into after they die.
what: VR experience combining LiDAR hologram depth video with photogrammetry to create a walkable memory of a family home. Captured with Azure Kinect and iPhone 12 LiDAR via Depthkit, integrated in Unity.
why: LiDAR holograms are glitchy and pixelated — imperfect like our own memories. This project used that imperfection as an emotional quality, not a technical limitation.

title: Hug
tagline: Ginkgo leaf digital sculpture — holding on and letting go
description: A digital sculpture about holding on and letting go.
what: Two people photographed hugging, photogrammetry-scanned into 3D meshes, decimated, and reconstructed in Rhino/Grasshopper with a custom script that tiles ginkgo leaves across every face of the mesh — each leaf oriented to the face normal and scripted to blow away in simulated wind. Multi-material resin print. Three earlier iterations were abandoned (subtracting one body to reveal an invisible hugger; stretching the legs to suggest precariousness; 3D printing the mesh wireframe) before the leaf system emerged as the form that could actually carry the emotion.
why: A hug is one of the most human things there is, and also one of the most impossible to hold onto. The ginkgo leaf — which drops all at once, suddenly, after holding on all season — became the formal language for this paradox: we try to keep what we love, but the trying is both foolish and impossible. Seeing beauty in temporariness is a clearer, kinder path.

title: RippleStar
tagline: Generative music visualizer with physics simulation
description: A generative audio-visual web experience where music drives a physics simulation of rippling star fields.
what: WebGL + Web Audio API. Audio input (mic or file) drives an FFT analysis that seeds particle emitters. Particles follow a custom gravitational simulation with spring forces. Fully configurable color and physics parameters.
why: Most music visualizers map frequency directly to visual parameters. This project used audio as a trigger for a simulation with its own dynamics — so the visuals have memory.

title: CinnaBot
tagline: Polar-coordinate drawing robot that deposits cinnamon patterns on cakes and coffee
description: A drawing robot with a polar gantry system that uses a vibrating end effector to deposit cinnamon in precise circles on cakes and coffee. PS70 group project with Ian Palk, Marcus, and Calvin.
what: A polar-coordinate drawing robot: a rotating platform on roller bearings controlled by a 12V stepper motor, with a linear screw motor moving a vibrating cinnamon-dispensing end effector along the radius. The cinnamon only deposits when the eccentric motor vibrates — the platform rotates a full circle while the radial position is fixed, drawing precise cinnamon rings. Built over a 4am all-night sprint in the PS70 lab. Group project with Ian Palk, Marcus, and Calvin.
why: My partner is a pastry chef — we wanted to make a machine that could draw with cinnamon on her cakes and coffee. Everyone was immediately on board with this completely unhinged idea.

title: PigeonBelt
tagline: Compass belt that vibrates when you face north — wearable wayfinding
description: A wearable belt module with an ESP32, BNO055 compass, and eccentric motor that vibrates subtly when the wearer faces north.
what: An ESP32 Xiao-based wearable module containing a Bosch BNO055 9-axis IMU for compass heading, an eccentric motor for haptic feedback, a li-po battery with USB charging, an LED indicator, and a custom Fusion 360 housing with a pigeon-embossed snap-on front face designed by Julia. The BNO055 outputs a calibrated Euler-angle yaw referenced to north — the motor buzzes once when the wearer faces north.
why: This has been an idea since I was ten years old — using felt sense rather than a screen for navigation, turning your understanding of urban space into something embodied. Twenty-six years later, here we are.

title: CNC Cheeseboard
tagline: Topographic cheese board of the Ojai valley milled on a ShopBot CNC
description: A topographic cheese board of the Ojai valley in California, where Wyatt's parents live, milled from oak on a ShopBot CNC using a TouchTerrain model.
what: Used TouchTerrain to generate an STL of the Ojai valley terrain, then milled it from an oak block on the ShopBot CNC. The mountain relief creates natural plateaus for cheese and charcuterie. Finished with butcher block conditioner. A prototype for a larger version using two-pass CNC with finer bit sizes for smoother mountain detail.
why: My parents live on the edge of a mountain in Ojai, California, and the shape of their valley seemed perfect for a cheese board — you could rest a little block of brie right in the valley floor.

title: Fan Lamp
tagline: Kinetic oscillating lamp built from laser-cut wood, custom gears, and a motor
description: A moving lamp made from laser-cut wood with a gear-driven mechanism that oscillates the fan blades, symbolizing the yin-yang ebb and flow of creating.
what: A kinetic desk lamp made from laser-cut wood with custom-designed gears that convert the motor's rotational movement into a windshield-wiper oscillation for the fan blades. Designed in Fusion 360, prototyped in cardboard, then lasercut in wood and assembled with screws and a small motor. Battery-powered with a soldered switch and USB light.
why: Fanlamp is a moving light that symbolizes the yin-yang ebb and flow process of life and creating — the orange side might mean stress, the white side peace; or the orange side motivated, the white fallow.

title: Food Chain
tagline: 3D-printed food chain pendant sketched in VR — organic forms from digital tools
description: A 3D-printed food chain of interlocking animals, sketched in VR using Adobe Medium and printed in high resolution on a Prusa printer.
what: Designed in VR using Adobe Medium — sketching interlocking animal and plant forms in 3D space — then exported as OBJ and prepared in Prusa slicer. After multiple failed prints at lower resolutions, succeeded at 0.05mm (9 hours overnight). Support removal required carefully heating a scalpel and using tiny pliers. Second iteration redrawn from scratch with 2D animal outlines extruded in 3D for less support material.
why: Exploring what happens when digital tools are used to create objects that feel organic and hand-made. 3D printers excel at interlocking forms, so a food chain — where each link must pass through the next — became the design challenge.

title: Windowsill Stamp
tagline: CNC-milled wax mold for casting a silicone stamp of the Windowsill Bakery logo
description: A CNC-milled wax mold used to cast a silicone stamp of a partner's bakery logo, made as a gift for her small business.
what: Converted the Windowsill Bakery hand-drawn logo into Blender paths, applied as a displacement map to a wax mold surface. CNC-milled using two bit sizes — 1/8" for bulk material removal, 1/16" for fine letterwork. Cast with equal-part A/B silicone, left to cure 16 hours, then trimmed and tested as a cake-box stamp.
why: My partner runs Windowsill Bakery and stamps all her custom cake boxes by hand. I'd wanted to make her a proper stamp for months but couldn't figure out the right workflow — CNC-milling wax for silicone casting turned out to be the answer.

title: Storytelling Affordances
tagline: Research on what spatial media affords that flat media cannot
description: A literature review and prototype study series isolating what spatial media can do that flat media cannot, examining presence, parallax, scale, and persistence as narrative affordances.
what: Literature review + 4 prototype studies. Each prototype isolates one affordance (presence, parallax, scale, persistence) and tests how it can carry narrative weight. Paper submitted to CHI 2025.
why: Spatial media is often described as 'more immersive' but that's not an affordance, it's a feeling. This research tried to be more precise.

title: Skybinder
tagline: Meditative VR puzzle game — connect dots into origami animals
description: A meditative VR puzzle game about connecting constellations of dots into origami animals, co-created with Luis Zanforlin at Maku XR.
what: A VR puzzle game built in Unity. Players connect numbered dots into triangles, filling in the faces of origami animals piece by piece — each dot tells you how many connections it needs, but not which. Developed and published by Maku XR. Reviewed by VRScout, 6DOF, UploadVR, and VR Focus.
why: Most VR games in 2021 relied on stress and violence. This project asked what the opposite would feel like — a game designed around calm and thoughtfulness, built from VR's fundamental building blocks: vertices, lines, and faces.

title: Painting Life
tagline: VR experience — step inside a painter's home and studio to understand her art
description: A VR experience built from photogrammetry scans of an artist's home and studio, where you navigate her spaces to understand the relationship between her life and her art.
what: A photogrammetry-based VR narrative experience built in Unity. Players navigate painter Emma Webster's home, studio, and garden, following one of her sculptures as a guide. Scanned in RealityCapture, cleaned in Adobe Medium and Blender. Awarded NewView VR Tokyo.
why: Visiting an artist's studio reveals context no gallery can provide. VR makes that intimate, exploratory encounter accessible — not as a documentary to watch but as a space you inhabit and move through on your own terms.

title: Sound AG
tagline: VR experience shrinking you into the soil microbiome to explain Sound Agriculture's technology
description: A commissioned VR experience for Sound Agriculture where you shrink into the soil microbiome to understand how their fertilizer-reducing technology works at the microbial level.
what: A commissioned VR experience for Sound Agriculture. You start in an Idaho cornfield, then shrink into the soil until you are the same size as the microbes that help plants grow — giving you a first-person view of the biology their product activates. Translated into Spanish and Portuguese.
why: Sound Agriculture's technology reduces fertilizer use significantly, but it works through invisible soil biology. Making the invisible literally walkable is more powerful than any diagram or explainer video.

title: Piece of String
tagline: VR photogrammetry piece — follow a string through a real home and the memories left behind
description: A VR experience built from photogrammetry of a real home, where a piece of string guides you through the spaces and the emotions left behind.
what: A VR experience built from DSLR photogrammetry scans of a real home, processed in RealityCapture, cleaned in Oculus Medium, and assembled in Unity. A piece of string guides you through the rooms, drawing attention to the emotional residue left in spaces by the people who lived in them. Awarded NewView International VR Award.
why: When a space is gone and only a digital record remains — one you can only enter through a headset — does it still carry the same emotional weight? This piece asks whether presence is architectural or psychological.

title: Ceramics + 3D
tagline: Workshop curriculum combining traditional ceramics with digital fabrication
description: A two-week workshop curriculum for design students combining wheel throwing and hand building with 3D scanning, digital modification, and clay 3D printing.
what: Workshop materials including session plans, equipment lists, project briefs, and reference images. Piloted with 16 MDes students. Students scan their hand-built forms, modify in Rhino, and print modifications back in clay.
why: Digital fabrication and craft are usually taught separately. This workshop tried to use each to critique the other.

title: Play Me
tagline: Live performance art on Twitch — the audience controlled my every action for 30 minutes
description: An experimental live performance on Twitch where an internet audience voted on Wyatt's every action for 30 minutes in real life.
what: A live Twitch performance in which an internet audience voted every 60 seconds on Wyatt's next action in real life. Built around a helmet-mounted iPhone rig streaming his POV and a Twitch bot compiling audience votes. The audience could suggest any action; he would do it within harm's discretion.
why: The hypothesis was that radical perspective-sharing and shared responsibility for decisions would produce empathy. Instead, deindividuation within the group produced the opposite — the audience treated Wyatt and everyone around him like NPCs, to humorous effect.

title: Falling Fall
tagline: Slow video meditation on autumn leaves and gravity
description: A 10-minute gallery video of autumn leaves filmed against a white sheet, presenting gravity and drag as the primary subjects.
what: 10-minute looping video, shot over three weeks in October 2020 in Cambridge. Leaves filmed against a white sheet in diffuse outdoor light. No color grading. Presented as a gallery loop.
why: Autumn is one of the most photographed seasons, always as spectacle. Slowing it down removes the spectacle and leaves just physics — the specific weight and drag of each leaf.

title: Canyons
tagline: Photogrammetry scans of urban street canyons as sculptural objects
description: A series of photogrammetric captures of Manhattan street canyons, processed into dense meshes and exhibited as 3D prints and interactive web objects.
what: 12 captures made walking through midtown Manhattan with a 360° camera rig. Processed in RealityCapture into ~50M polygon meshes. Decimated versions presented as interactive WebGL objects; full meshes 3D printed at 1:1000 scale.
why: The street canyon is an accidental architecture — built bottom-up by zoning and capitalism. Photogrammetry makes it an object you can hold.

title: haaaard
tagline: Physical fitness app built around honesty instead of optimization
description: An iOS fitness app that replaces metrics and streaks with a single daily question: how hard did you move today?
what: iOS app (Swift). No step counts, no streaks, no graphs. Each day: a single question ('How hard did you move today?') with a 1–5 slider. Weekly: a reflection prompt. Annual: a single summary number.
why: Fitness apps create anxiety about the metrics rather than relationship with the body. This one tried to replace the dashboard with a question.

title: 3D Printing for Designers
tagline: Introductory 3D printing curriculum for Harvard GSD
description: Three-session introductory 3D printing curriculum for Harvard GSD, starting from design intent and working back to machine operation.
what: Three 3-hour sessions: modeling for print (Rhino), slicing and printer operation (Prusa MK3S), and finishing and post-processing. Includes tutorials, exercises, and a final project brief.
why: FabLab inductions often teach tool operation without design context. This curriculum started from design intent and worked back to machine operation.

title: Harvard Memorial
tagline: Proposal for a student-led memorial to Harvard's enslaved laborers
description: A student-led design proposal for a Harvard memorial to enslaved laborers, developed through community workshops and submitted to the Presidential Task Force on Slavery.
what: Design process: 8 student workshops, 3 faculty interviews, archival research. Proposal includes spatial concept, material palette, programming, and maintenance plan. Submitted to Harvard's Presidential Task Force on Slavery.
why: Harvard's memorial planning has been faculty and administrator-led. This proposal insisted that students — especially Black students — should lead the design.

title: Paper Lamps
tagline: Handmade paper lamps using traditional Japanese techniques
description: An 8-lamp series made from handmade kozo fiber paper processed in a home studio, exploring how washi transmits light differently from glass or plastic.
what: 8-lamp series. Paper made from kozo fiber processed in a home studio. Frames bent from mild steel rod. Fitted with Edison bulbs and simple toggle switches.
why: Washi transmits light differently from glass or plastic — warmer, more directional, with visible texture. This series tried to make that difference the point.

title: Galapagos
tagline: Stanford documentary — chasing wonder at the edge of the Galápagos
description: A short documentary about Stanford students and alumni learning together in the Galápagos Islands.
what:
Stanford education extends beyond the classroom, and beyond your twenties.

Our panga bounced in the choppy water. We'd caught two flights, a bus, another flight, another bus, a ferry, another bus, and were finally at our destination — an abandoned archipelago in the middle of the Pacific. The isolation of the Galápagos is its source of power; but also renders it devastatingly fragile.

Twelve Sophomore undergrads were there for a class called Darwin, Evolution, Galápagos. 30 alumni were there for a Continued Learning vacation. The professor was there to make sure everyone learned and stayed alive.
why: Stanford asked me to capture its community's love of learning as seen through the frame of this magical wilderness.

title: PhilDev
tagline: Design strategy for a Philippine development nonprofit
description: A six-month design strategy engagement with PhilDev, a Philippine development nonprofit, covering stakeholder interviews, brand refresh, and measurement framework.
what: 6-month engagement: stakeholder interviews, theory of change mapping, communications audit, brand refresh (identity system + web redesign), and measurement framework. Implemented by PhilDev staff.
why: Development nonprofits often have strong programs and weak communications. This engagement tried to close that gap — making the work legible to funders and beneficiaries alike.

title: SOLS
tagline: Custom orthotics using 3D scanning and parametric design
description: A custom orthotic workflow using iPhone LiDAR scanning and parametric Grasshopper modeling, reducing cost from $400–800 to ~$60 and turnaround to 3 days.
what: iPhone LiDAR scan → custom Python processing → Rhino/Grasshopper parametric model → SLS print in TPU. Prototype fit-tested with 8 participants over 6 weeks.
why: Custom orthotics cost $400–$800 and require specialist visits. This workflow brought the cost to ~$60 and the turnaround to 3 days.

title: Calm
tagline: Video ad for Calm — what if the commercial could itself be a meditation?
description: A commissioned video for the Calm meditation app, designed so that the commercial itself functions as a small taste of the product.
what: A video commissioned for Calm, the meditation app. The creative brief was nature-focused; the production process involved extended meditation research. The resulting video is designed so that watching it offers a glimpse of the calm the app promises.
why: Calm's brand is defined by nature and restraint. The insight was that a commercial for a relaxation app could itself be a small act of relaxation — making the form match the product.

title: OffGridBox
tagline: Design research for off-grid energy/water systems in rural communities
description: A short film about the people and technology bringing off-grid energy to rural Rwanda.
what: A short documentary film about OffGridBox, a company bringing containerized solar and water purification systems to off-grid communities in Rwanda. Made after visiting the country and seeing the impact firsthand.
why: Something amazing is happening in Rwanda — many amazing things. I made this film about one of them.

title: BufferZone
tagline: Documentary — life at the edge of a Nepali jungle, where humans and wildlife share a border
description: A documentary about life at the edge of a Nepali national park, where soldiers protect rhinos, women sell solar light, and humans and wildlife share an uneasy border.
what:
A documentary about the buffer zone at the edge of Chitwan National Park, Nepal — where ordinary people live alongside wild animals, the army protects rhinos from illegal poaching, and women move out of poverty by selling solar light.

Created for two American nonprofits: Empowered By Light and Empower Generation.
why:
My brief was to make a three-minute mini-documentary. Two trips, four weeks of shooting, and hundreds of hours of footage later, I realized that this many perspectives required more time.

Director: Wyatt Roy · Producer: Alyssa Newman · Pre-production: Arthur Scott, Ashley Gremmel

title: StreetLogic
tagline: Launch video for StreetLogic — an AI-powered smart dashcam for cyclists
description: A launch video for StreetLogic, an AI-powered smart dashcam for cyclists that warns when cars get too close.
what: A launch video made for StreetLogic, a smart bike dashcam that records 360° footage and uses AI to warn cyclists when vehicles approach dangerously. Made to help spread the vision of safe urban cycling to cyclists and investors.
why: I love cycling in cities and the danger is real. StreetLogic needed to sell a dream as much as a product — safety on streets that are already hostile to cyclists.

title: Peloton
tagline: Video for a trucking safety company — helped land Bloomberg coverage and raise a funding round
description: A brand video for Peloton Technology, a trucking safety company, that helped them land Bloomberg News coverage and raise their next funding round.
what: A brand and technology video for Peloton Technology — a trucking safety company (no relation to the bike brand) whose AI-assisted convoy systems reduce accidents and fuel consumption. The video helped secure Bloomberg News coverage and close the company's next funding round.
why: Trucking technology is life-and-death but its audiences — fleet managers, investors, regulators — are not easily moved by specs. Video that makes the human and safety stakes vivid is more persuasive than any data sheet.

title: StoryMaker
tagline: Hero's Journey bedtime story companion with random character prompts
description: A web tool that helps you ad-lib a complete Hero's Journey bedtime story with randomly generated character prompts for names, fears, objects, and settings.
what: A step-by-step web tool that walks you through the stages of the Hero's Journey to help you improvise a complete bedtime story. Each stage gives you random prompts — character names starting with certain letters, their biggest fears, favorite objects, homelands — to spark your imagination. You type or say the story aloud as you click through each stage. The site generates the structure and prompts; the narrative drama is up to you.
why: It's hard to ad-lib a proper bedtime story with a complete hero's journey arc. I made this simple tool to help you come up with one on the spot. All the narrative drama is still up to you — but these are the bones. It'll even give you some help with names and relationships to get your ideas flowing.

title: All RGB
tagline: Image containing every 24-bit RGB color exactly once
description: A 4096×4096 pixel image containing all 16,777,216 possible 24-bit RGB colors exactly once, arranged using a Hilbert curve space-filling algorithm.
what: Python script generates the image using a Hilbert curve to minimize perceptual distance between adjacent pixels. Printed at 40"×40" on metallic paper. Also available as an interactive web viewer.
why: The full RGB color space is usually encountered as an abstract swatch. This project made it a picture — all of color, at once, in one place.
